sentences.text: One of the first hidden schools, the ogrodek, that had a more structured academic program, was in a garden.

There wasn't-- well, first maybe, if I may jump a little bit back, back up a little bit-- I did not point out that one of the things that occurred rather early after the invasion was that Jewish people were restricted from living in certain areas.
On our street, there was an estate that I guess was vacated.
And it had-- it had-- a-- it was rather large, and it was wooded.
It had trees.
And inside that-- that area, there was a little building, and that was the school.
And this is also one of the things that just stands out in my memory as such an important, such a nurturing time, because, well, the teachers were so remarkable.
They were so kind.
They-- they even gave us plots for us to grow vegetables, to grow flowers.
That was a place where we could really laugh and be children and played games, lots of ballgames, art, literature was shared with such-- with such affection, with such kindness.
But that, too, was stopped after a short time.
These teachers are so vivid in my mind.
Each one was like a parent to me, a parent, an educator in the noblest, noblest sense.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that, too, stopped.
As I'm thinking about it, I remember them reading to us.
I remember that instilling that love for literature, that-- that it was more than pages, that these were expressions that sprang from the soul.
And that was very precious.
That, too, as I said, was-- was stopped, and I don't know exactly the circumstances.
I'm sure that there were lots that I cannot think of right now.
But we were-- everything was kind of-- we were-- it was-- we were constantly at the mercy and whim of the Germans.

I-- please remind me of it if I-- if I omit it, because they did happen after the-- after the ghetto was-- after the wall was built, probably more so they became-- some of the things that happened that will describe that.
So I'll get back to that, and please don't let me forget.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then-- then I don't recall what accounted for the closing of that ogrodek.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had tutors.
And that was very, very common.

You saw-- often saw parents who were swollen with hunger, and I don't remember if I mentioned the bread was rationed, that they really traded their meager rations so that they can use it in exchange pay for the children to be tutored.
For us, going to school was-- again, it was a sign of resistance.
It was-- it was a display of resistance.
We did not live just by bread alone, literally.

That was I think the most graphic, the most real examples that were just not isolated examples but just were very common is-- is-- is going to-- having the tutors and-- and exchanging a crumb of bread for schooling.
Yeah, yeah, yes.
As I was saying, that was a sign of resistance.
And in some ways the children were very much involved, too, because when we went to-- to-- when we went to our hidden tutors, we had to hide our books.
Books were contraband, forbidden.
And so we had-- we carried our books under our clothes.
And we knew perfectly well that if we would be caught, then that our parents would be hung or shot or killed instantly.
So we did that at a very, very great risk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Right.
Right, right.
We even had tutors in the bunkers, in our subterranean existence.
And I'll touch upon that.
Oh, you asked me about-- about if there were any displays or any anti-Semitism shown by the Poles outside the ghetto.
See, we were isolated.
We were in a ghetto, so there was no direct contact.
However, there were some Jews who-- who tried to pass as non-Jews.
We had naby of-- many people had false papers.
And the way they displayed their hostility towards us was that you had some people, some Poles, whose occupation was to smell out, to snuff out the Jews.
Now, if you'd look at the people, there was no physical distinction-- the ones who tried to pass as non-Jews.
There was no physical distinction.
But if you-- what they did, they stared in your eyes.
And it was just, you know, like-- if you were afraid, if you blinked they became so sharp in catching that glint, that moment of panic, of fear.
And everyone was aware of it, so people practiced.
It was like a lie detector, you know?
It was so subtle.
But they were like dogs.
So I had a couple of friends who were also hidden.
And-- and I think it's important to remember the few righteous ones.
But they were not very common.
It was much more common-- it was much more common to be rejected and to be betrayed, but also occasionally you had some who were hiding.
Can you tell me about the-- the formation of the ghetto and the building of the wall?

The formation of the ghetto-- in a way-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the formation of the ghetto, in a way, occurred right away, because you were-- I think the wall was the most visible and the most tangible and the most-- I mean, you can point at it even today.
I doubt that there is any piece left, but that is very easy to imagine.
But actually, the formation of the ghetto started the minute the Germans came in, the minute they marched in, because you were immediately identified.
You-- immediately certain streets were forbidden for Jews and certain streets were designated.
So even though there was not a wall, of course, the wall marked it off.
And I don't recall when they started the wall, whether it was in '41.
I think it was in '41.
I'm not sure.
But that to us, also it did make an impression, because right around the walls, you had the Germans stationed.
So the-- the fact that we were so boxed in became much more-- much more visible to us, not that we didn't know it.
And of course, we were afraid to come too close to it.
And of course, then also getting-- getting through for some people to either go there for hiding.
Or-- you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or to go there for food, also became much more difficult, because it was harder to get through.
I think that there was some Polish guards, too.
There were Lithuanians.
You know that the Germans enlisted the help of Lithuanians and Ukrainians, and I think-- and Poles.
And I think during a time like that, it gives a lot of occupations and a lot of meaning to a lot of sadists, and [LAUGHS] there were quite a few.
None.
None that I am aware of.
Well, they-- they began to deteriorate very markedly from the very beginning, because quite clearly a lot of normal life was totally disrupted.
Food became a problem and starvation as-- as time advanced.
Whatever littler-- there was a black market.
And-- and there were some people who-- for instance, my father was a jeweler.
We were able to conceal some of our gold.
You know, gold has, especially during the war, the value-- gold and probably some gems.
And so-- and my father did some trading.
And so there were some-- like we were-- and as I said, we were not uprooted.
But-- but generally, these resources, too, became fewer and fewer.
And so hunger and starvation, a lot of death, epidemics-- oh, typhoid was rampant, and so a lot of death.
We were just so boxed in, so-- so quarantined.
And so there was-- there was not enough water, not enough clothing.
And now you can visualize this-- this enclosed area.
And everyday, people are flooded in from the surrounding areas.
No place to go, so you had people just-- just littering the streets.
And of course, during the winter, during the-- Poland is quite cold.
And-- and when you're hungry, you're even colder.
There was no fuel.
There was no light.
And-- and progressively it became worse and worse, and the raids and all of that kept on increasing.
Also the threshold-- people were more worn out.
So the threshold, you know, just the physical appearance became dimmer and dimmer and dimmer.
And hope became much more remote.
No.
No, I mean, my father kind of, you know, worked, and-- which was all very clandestine.
And we-- no, and the rest-- we tried to keep it as normal as we could.
Within our family, our home, our family was like a little capsule, like-- like a little safe haven.
You know, I have these images with the flickering carbide, where everything was dark.
The windows were blacked out, and the silence-- the silence beyond our walls were deafening.
Because, you know, we listened to the silence, because it often-- there were clues.
Survival depended on alertness.
So you always listened to the silence, if anything would erupt.
But in our-- I remember our kitchen, we would sit, of course, only at one place, because you could only keep one place warm, if you had enough fuel.
And we had the carbide light.
And my father would read.
He read to us a lot-- [HEBREW] and Sholem Aleichem and Sholem Asch.
And he just brought back the images of-- of a world that-- that fed us.
But outside, outside the quiet and the threat always hung.
Now, our-- our apartment, our windows were directly across the-- across the courtyard from the gate.
Now, it was my sister's and my job to often watch if the-- because you never knew where the Germans would just spring out from nowhere.
And so it was sort of our job to watch if the Germans were coming into the building.
It was also all the children's job when we were playing outside to keep-- nobody told us, but we were part of the family.
My parents-- all the discussions and everything was shared, and we knew where things were hidden and everything.
Right.
They were treated as kindly as they possibly could.
You know, I-- as I said, there were the [INAUDIBLE].
And I'll tell you-- please don't let me forget about Janusz Korczak, who is one example of how one Jew-- how we treated one another.
I know that there are lots of people who talk about kapos, and there was a Jewish police force.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- I think that the Jews were often-- see, my recollections of all of that, of the kapos, with-- with the exception of a few very, very, very isolated ones-- is that they were not unkind, that-- that-- in a way, they-- they-- they were designated by or requested-- all right, the Jews in the ghetto, the Jewish community was-- was called on to form a police force.
Well, you know, you-- you think of what the choices were.
Well, you know, either-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think only maybe a war prisoner can understand that in order to rebel physically you-- that something has to be ripe.
But really, we did not-- at this point, there was still hope that that will pass.
Now, you look at your children, and you say, OK, I'll go out, and I'm going to stab a-- someone, a German, which one of my uncles did.
But it was at the moment when his wife and daughter were shot.
But prior to that, you don't want to risk your child.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yes, I'd like to return to that.
So I must also say that maybe the one who starts the fire is responsible, you know?
And really they did.
The fire was started by those who demanded them.
And the option for the-- for the-- for the Jewish community was-- well, first of all, we had no option.
But our police certainly was much safer than having the Germans.
Sure, anything, just stay out there.
Now, but the police also, in order to protect the Jewish community, had to, in a way-- they had-- they had in a way, to show that they are doing their job.
So they had to maintain a certain kind of order, as prescribed by-- by the Germans.
But I have never-- I personally, have never experienced them to accommodate it, other than just going through the motion of OK, you know, we are a soldier.
We are police.
We're doing our job.
But in most cases-- I remember one isolated case, when we were in concentration camp, when a policeman, a Jewish kapo hit-- hit a few Jews.
And for each one that you saw this, you always had a counter example.

My mother, who was a little skinny thing at that time-- she was shorter than I am-- and was swollen [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with hunger, barefoot, just wooden clogs that happened in the winter, and she forgot herself.
She just jumped out, and she said, you are a Jew.
How can you hit-- hit him?
I mean, the German was standing right-- that she wasn't shot was a miracle.
But she could not-- so for-- she could not be silent.
But, as I said, there were kitchens organized.
And people were fed as much as possible.
Maybe, you know-- yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Dr.
Korczak.
Dr.
Korczak-- you know, we all need heroes.
And, you know how my-- I guess it's the healthy part in me.
Dr.
Korczak just was my hero.
I have known of Dr.
Korczak when I was very young, before the war.
He was a pedagogue.
He was a doctor.
And his mission in life was loving children.
His mission in love-- in life was to be a parent to orphans.
And he ran orphanages.
I loved him.
My first love was through books.
He wrote books about-- all my friends read them.
I think every Polish child read them.
In his books, the heroes were children, and they became kings, and it was like turning tables.
And the world was run by children.
And we-- usually, during the summer, we would go to the country.
And one year we went to the country to a camp, a children's camp.
And there was a counselor who told us about-- she was an orphan and she was raised in his orphanage.
So when she talked about Dr.
Korczak, she talked about her father.
And she said, oh-- and, you know, I remember, we were wondering how could he be a father?
We came from families of one, two, three, four maybe.
But how could he be a father to so many children?
And she said, oh, he was.
And I remember one example that really registered with me, and that was she said every time a child was sick or had a loose tooth, they always ran to Dr.
Korczak, because if Dr.
Korczak pulled the tooth, it absolutely didn't hurt.
So you can see the love that he imparted and the love that the children felt for him.
Well, during the war, Dr.
Korczak still stayed in the ghetto with the orphanage.
And he was trying to raise money.
And I remember my sister and I helped by-- there were also little children's theaters.
And we performed in the little-- in the children's theaters, and the funds went to the orphanage, to Dr.
Korczak.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The last that I saw of Dr.
Korczak was that at some point, when-- during the liquidation of the Warsaw ghetto, that I will come back to a little bit later, Dr.
Korczak-- an ordinance came out that the orphanage has to be delivered to Treblinka.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and then there was a last-minute attempt still to save Dr.
Korczak, because he knew that these children are-- have to be delivered to Treblinka.
Now, he made a choice.
He made several choices.
One of his choice was how to do it most mercifully for the children.
There was no way to hide them.
So what he did-- what he did was he told the children that they are going to-- on a-- on a-- on an outing, on a picnic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He gave them flags, and singing the 'Hatikvah,' he marched right into the office of Treblinka with them.
[CRYING]
